metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
verify command `wc -l` inspects output with `wc` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Provide concrete anchored grep patterns for AC5's three required additions, e.g. `grep -E 'REBAR_USAGE_LOG'`, `grep -F 'usage_log summarize'`, and `grep -F 'upload-artifact'`, each scoped to the correct workflow file.
evidence: 'grep for the three additions' — no pattern, flag, or anchor is given
The three additions (REBAR_USAGE_LOG env, summarize step, upload-artifact step) each have distinct YAML keys that could be anchored
findings.finding: AC5 directs the executor to 'grep for the three additions' in each workflow YAML but specifies no grep pattern, leaving the proving command under-specified (verify-command taxonomy defect #1: no anchored pattern). A grep that matches a substring without word-boundary anchoring can produce a false positive.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria §5 — 'verified by `yaml.safe_load` of each file and grep for the three additions'
scenarios: An executor writes a grep pattern that matches a comment or unrelated line; the AC passes on a structurally valid but behaviorally incorrect YAML.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace 'grep for the three additions' with three explicit commands:
```
grep -E 'REBAR_USAGE_LOG' .github/workflows/prompt-eval.yml
grep -F 'usage_log summarize' .github/workflows/prompt-eval.yml
grep -F 'upload-artifact' .github/workflows/prompt-eval.yml
```
(and mirror for external-integration.yml).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test (and a matching AC bullet) that verifies `record(...)` silently swallows a write error (e.g. by pointing `REBAR_USAGE_LOG` at a read-only path or monkeypatching `open`) and does not raise.
evidence: "Best-effort: it wraps its file write in a `try/except` and never raises into the LLM call path (a telemetry sink must not break a run)."
Testing section lists four test scenarios; none mention error-suppression or simulating a write failure.
AC1 says only 'verified by `pytest tests/unit/test_usage_log.py` passing' with no mention of the error-path.
findings.finding: The `record` function's best-effort error-suppression behaviour ("wraps its file write in a try/except and never raises") is described in Approach step 1 but has no corresponding unit test and no AC that covers it. If someone later removes the try/except, no test will catch the regression and a disk-full or permissions error could break a live LLM run.
findings.location: Approach step 1 (`usage_log.py` description); Testing section; AC1
scenarios: A future refactor removes the try/except; a disk-full condition in CI then causes the weekly LLM run to abort instead of completing and still uploading results.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Testing section, add: 'Error-suppression: call `record(...)` with `REBAR_USAGE_LOG` set to a path that cannot be written (e.g. a directory, or monkeypatch `builtins.open` to raise `OSError`) and assert no exception propagates.' Mirror this in AC2 or as a new AC bullet.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief doc comment block (or AGENTS.md entry) to `usage_log.py` capturing: the module's role as the opt-in process-level telemetry sink, why Langfuse/log-parsing were rejected, and how to extend it (e.g. add cost columns when a price table exists).
evidence: Plan introduces `src/rebar/llm/usage_log.py` as a new module with its own CLI, CI integration, and artifact upload — a non-trivial new pipeline stage
Plan explicitly contrasts this approach against the Langfuse path and documents the rejected alternatives inline, but only in the PR description, not in any persistent doc
No mention of AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
The 'Rejected alternatives' rationale (why not Langfuse, why not log-parsing) is valuable context that will be lost once the PR description ages out
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new architectural pattern (process-level JSONL telemetry sink, CI job-summary integration, artifact upload) with no update planned for AGENTS.md, an ADR, or any design doc — future maintainers have no discoverable record of why this module exists, how to extend it, or how it relates to the existing Langfuse tracing path.
scenarios: A future developer adding a new LLM provider or job may not discover usage_log.py and duplicate effort
Someone extending the sink (e.g. adding cost columns) has no doc explaining the deliberate decision to omit pricing
The Langfuse vs. JSONL architectural decision is undocumented in the repo
findings.suggested_fix: At the top of `src/rebar/llm/usage_log.py`, add a module docstring (5–10 lines) summarising: 'opt-in JSONL sink for token usage; activated by REBAR_USAGE_LOG env var; one record per PydanticAIRunner.run call; Langfuse and log-parsing rejected because [reasons from Approach section]; extend by adding columns here and updating summarize()'. Optionally add a one-liner to AGENTS.md pointing to this module for token-usage observability.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `record()` assumes single-process access (and document the constraint) or uses a file lock (e.g., `fcntl.flock` / `msvcrt.locking`) to make concurrent appends safe.
evidence: Plan says 'a single hook covers both without any per-test or per-eval plumbing' — implies single-process, but does not assert or enforce this
No file-locking or atomic-rename is mentioned in the `record()` spec
CONCURRENCY SAFETY sub-criterion: shared/mutable state must be mutated atomically
findings.finding: The plan is silent on concurrent-write safety for the shared JSONL file. If the test suite that populates `REBAR_USAGE_LOG` is ever run with pytest-xdist (parallel workers), multiple processes will open-append to the same file simultaneously. POSIX `O_APPEND` write atomicity is only guaranteed up to `PIPE_BUF` bytes (~4 KB); a JSON line exceeding that limit, or a non-POSIX filesystem (Windows runner), can produce interleaved/corrupted JSONL that `summarize` cannot parse.
findings.location: Approach §2 — runner.py seam; Testing — 'drive PydanticAIRunner.run through the existing offline FunctionModel model-override path'
scenarios: pytest-xdist is added to the repo later for speed; both external and eval-live jobs silently start writing corrupted JSONL
Windows GitHub-hosted runner used for eval-live job; O_APPEND atomicity not guaranteed, interleaved writes produce unparseable lines
findings.suggested_fix: For the stated single-process CI use case, add a one-line comment in `record()` and the plan: 'Not safe for concurrent multi-process use; callers must ensure a single writer.' This documents the constraint without adding complexity for the current scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test in `test_usage_log.py` that points `REBAR_USAGE_LOG` at a non-writable path (or patches `open` to raise `OSError`) and asserts `record(...)` returns without raising, verifying the try/except safety contract.
evidence: Plan states: 'Best-effort: it wraps its file write in a try/except and never raises into the LLM call path (a telemetry sink must not break a run).'
Test plan lists: record appends, record no-ops when unset, summarize aggregates, summarize on missing/empty, runner seam, CLI — no error-injection test.
The runner is a user-facing library API; an uncaught exception from `record` would surface to callers.
findings.finding: The plan explicitly promises `record()` "never raises into the LLM call path" (a safety contract for a shared runner), but no unit test verifies this: there is no test that injects a file-write failure (e.g., non-writable path, disk-full simulation) and asserts the exception is swallowed and the surrounding call completes normally.
scenarios: File at REBAR_USAGE_LOG path becomes non-writable mid-run (permissions change, disk full, read-only mount in a CI ephemeral container) — `record` raises, propagating into `PydanticAIRunner.run`, breaking the LLM call unexpectedly.
findings.suggested_fix:
In `test_usage_log.py`, add a test like:
```python
def test_record_swallows_write_error(monkeypatch, tmp_path):
    log_path = tmp_path / 'usage.jsonl'
    monkeypatch.setenv('REBAR_USAGE_LOG', str(log_path))
    with unittest.mock.patch('builtins.open', side_effect=OSError('disk full')):
        # Must not raise
        usage_log.record({'input_tokens': 1, 'output_tokens': 1,
                          'cache_read_tokens': 0, 'cache_write_tokens': 0,
                          'requests': 1}, op='test')
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Pin `actions/upload-artifact` in the new steps to the full commit SHA (e.g. `@043fb46d1a93c77aae656e7c1c64a875d1fc6a0a  # v7.0.1`) to match the repo's SHA-pinning practice in `release.yml`.
evidence: external-integration.yml:153: `uses: actions/upload-artifact@v7` (floating tag)
prompt-eval.yml:115,122: `uses: actions/upload-artifact@v7` (floating tag)
release.yml:121: `uses: actions/upload-artifact@043fb46d1a93c77aae656e7c1c64a875d1fc6a0a  # v7.0.1` (SHA-pinned)
Partially-verified: v7 action exists and works, but floating tag is inconsistent with repo security posture
findings.finding: The two new `actions/upload-artifact` steps in `external-integration.yml` (line 153) and `prompt-eval.yml` (lines 115, 122) use the floating tag `@v7` instead of a pinned commit SHA, inconsistent with the repo's own SHA-pinning practice visible in `release.yml` (which pins `@043fb46d1a93c77aae656e7c1c64a875d1fc6a0a # v7.0.1` for every upload-artifact use). A floating tag can be silently redirected to a different commit.
scenarios: A tag reassignment by the action maintainer (or a supply-chain compromise) silently changes the action executed in the two billable weekly jobs without any diff in the workflow file.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `uses: actions/upload-artifact@v7` with `uses: actions/upload-artifact@043fb46d1a93c77aae656e7c1c64a875d1fc6a0a  # v7.0.1` in both `external-integration.yml` and `prompt-eval.yml`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `_call_label` in the plan: state what variable or expression in `runner.py` provides the `op` value for `usage_log.record()` (e.g. the model name, the prompt-id, or a constant like the test node id), so an executor can act without inspecting live code.
evidence: 'call `usage_log.record(usage, op=_call_label)`' — `_call_label` appears nowhere else in the plan
The runner.py INFO log at 452-470 presumably contains an operation label, but the plan does not name the variable, its scope, or its value
findings.finding: The plan calls `usage_log.record(usage, op=_call_label)` at runner.py:486 but never defines or describes `_call_label` — its type, source (local variable, method, attribute, or constant), or how it identifies the call. An executor reading only the plan cannot determine what value to pass as `op` without inspecting the live codebase.
scenarios: Executor searches runner.py for `_call_label`, finds it absent, and must invent or guess a label — producing an op field whose meaning is undefined and potentially inconsistent with the per-op breakdown in `summarize()`.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence such as: '`op` should be set to `<expression>` — e.g., `prompt_id` if available at that call site, otherwise the model identifier string — so the per-op summary table groups calls meaningfully.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the artifact `name:` for each upload step in the plan (e.g. `usage-jsonl-eval-live` and `usage-jsonl-external`) so an executor does not have to invent names and collision risk is eliminated.
evidence: 'upload the raw JSONL via `actions/upload-artifact`' — no artifact `name:` field specified
Both the eval-live and external jobs produce a usage.jsonl; without distinct names, artifact collision is possible
findings.finding: The `actions/upload-artifact` step is specified without a name for the artifact, which is a required field in that action. An executor must invent a name, and if two jobs upload artifacts with the same auto-chosen name in the same workflow run, GitHub Actions will either fail or merge them unexpectedly.
scenarios: Both weekly jobs happen to run on the same trigger; artifacts collide or an executor reuses the same name across both YAMLs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach §3: 'Upload with `name: usage-jsonl-${{ github.job }}`' (or distinct static names per job) and `path: ${{ runner.temp }}/usage.jsonl`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete example of the expected `summarize()` Markdown output (even a minimal 2-column table stub) to the plan so the CLI unit test has a canonical string to assert against.
evidence: 'a totals row (summed tokens + total calls) plus a per-`op` breakdown table' — column names absent
'verified by the CLI test' — but the test can only assert the output equals some expected string if the expected string is known
findings.finding: The `summarize()` Markdown output format — column names, column order, and exact table syntax — is not specified in the plan. The CLI AC says 'prints the Markdown totals + per-op table' and the unit test checks 'correct totals and a per-op breakdown', but neither pins the schema. A reviewer or executor cannot write a precise assertion without knowing the expected column headers.
findings.location: Approach §1 / Acceptance Criteria §4 / Testing section — CLI test
scenarios: Two implementations produce different column orderings (e.g., input_tokens vs output_tokens first); both pass a loose 'contains a table' assertion but the artifact is inconsistent across runs.
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Approach §1 a sample output block, e.g.:
```markdown
| op | input | output | cache_read | cache_write | requests |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TOTAL** | 1200 | 400 | 0 | 0 | 3 |
| embed | 800 | 200 | 0 | 0 | 2 |
| chat | 400 | 200 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
```

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define what `_call_label` is in runner.py (or rename it to match an existing variable), and describe in the Approach how the `op` value is derived (e.g., model name, prompt-template ID, test node ID) so the per-op breakdown table in `summarize` is meaningful.
evidence: "call `usage_log.record(usage, op=_call_label)` (module-level import)"
Context describes runner.py at lines 392, 394, 452-470, 486 with no mention of `_call_label`
`summarize` produces a 'per-op breakdown table' making `op` semantically significant to users
findings.finding: The Approach (step 2) instructs the implementer to call `usage_log.record(usage, op=_call_label)` but `_call_label` is never defined or described anywhere in the plan. The Context section describes runner.py internals (usage extraction at line 392/394, INFO log at 452-470, result assignment at 486) but makes no mention of a `_call_label` variable. If this name doesn't exist in runner.py, the implementer must invent both the variable name and the logic for what label to assign — yet the per-op breakdown in `summarize` makes the `op` value meaningful to end users, so this is a load-bearing detail.
findings.location: Approach step 2 (`runner.py` edit); Context section describing runner.py internals
scenarios: Implementer opens runner.py, searches for `_call_label`, finds nothing, and must guess whether to use the model name, the test name, a prompt ID, or some other label — choices that change what the summary table communicates.
The per-op breakdown in the job summary becomes misleading or useless if `op` always receives a constant or an incorrect label.
findings.suggested_fix: In Approach step 2, replace `op=_call_label` with a concrete description such as: 'pass `op` as the model identifier (e.g. `result["model"]` or the `model_name` parameter already present in `PydanticAIRunner.__init__`)' and confirm the variable name matches what exists in runner.py at line 486.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ensure `record()`'s except clause emits at least a `warnings.warn(...)` or `logging.getLogger(__name__).warning(...)` so that a file-write failure is observable (appears in pytest output or CI log) without raising into the call path.
evidence: Plan says 'never raises into the LLM call path' and 'Best-effort' but specifies no logging of the caught exception
OBSERVABILITY sub-criterion: new failure points must be instrumented with a metric/log/trace/alert so operators can see and debug them
Silent swallowing means a misconfigured `REBAR_USAGE_LOG` path produces zero artifact and 'No LLM calls recorded.' indistinguishable from a run with zero LLM calls
findings.finding: When `record()`'s try/except catches a write error (e.g., unwritable path, disk full, bad `REBAR_USAGE_LOG` value), the failure is silently swallowed with no log, metric, or warning emitted. Operators running the weekly jobs have no way to know that usage recording is broken — the job appears to succeed but produces an empty/missing artifact and a misleading 'No LLM calls recorded.' summary.
findings.location: Approach §1 — `usage_log.py` `record()` spec: 'Best-effort: wraps file write in try/except and never raises'
scenarios: REBAR_USAGE_LOG points to a non-existent directory — every `record()` call silently fails, artifact is empty, summary reads 'No LLM calls recorded.' masking a config bug
Disk quota exceeded mid-run — first N calls recorded, rest silently dropped, totals are wrong with no indication
findings.suggested_fix: In the except block, add: `import logging; logging.getLogger(__name__).warning('usage_log.record failed: %s', e)`. This respects the 'never raise' contract while satisfying the observability requirement; the `NullHandler` on the rebar logger means it's a no-op in library use but visible when a basicConfig is active (as in CI).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `summarize()` skips (and optionally warns on) lines that fail `json.loads`, so a truncated final record from an abrupt job termination does not crash the summary step.
evidence: Spec enumerates only two non-happy paths for summarize: missing file and empty file
Partial/corrupt JSONL line (job killed mid-write, SIGKILL, runner OOM) is not mentioned
CI step runs `if: always()` after a potentially-failing test suite, so abrupt termination during record() is a realistic path
findings.finding: `summarize(path)` is spec'd to handle a missing or empty file but is not specified to handle malformed or partial JSONL (e.g., a JSON line truncated because the CI job was killed mid-write, or a future caller writing a non-JSON line). An unhandled `json.JSONDecodeError` would crash the `python -m rebar.llm.usage_log summarize` step in CI, causing the summary step itself to fail — even when the actual test/eval run succeeded.
findings.location: Approach §1 — `usage_log.py` `summarize(path)` spec; AC line: '`python -m rebar.llm.usage_log summarize <path>` prints the Markdown totals + per-op table (or `No LLM calls recorded.` for an empty/missing file)'
scenarios: GitHub Actions runner is killed mid-run (OOM, timeout) leaving a half-written final JSON line; subsequent `summarize` call raises JSONDecodeError and the summary step exits non-zero
A future caller writes a line with extra whitespace or a BOM — sufficient to break naive `json.loads` per line
findings.suggested_fix: In `summarize`, wrap each line parse in `try: row = json.loads(line) except json.JSONDecodeError: continue` so partial lines are silently skipped; optionally count skipped lines and append a footnote to the Markdown output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the plan with the current tree: usage_log.py, the runner.py import+record() call (lines 25, 491), and both workflow edits (REBAR_USAGE_LOG env + summarize step + upload-artifact) ALREADY EXIST — either drop the ticket as implemented, or restate it against the actual baseline and specify the concrete remaining delta (and cite any prerequisite that created these files with `[rebar:<C>]`).
evidence: Edit-set marks 'src/rebar/llm/usage_log.py' as '*new*', but the file already exists and its record()/summarize()/__main__ CLI match the described semantics verbatim (record no-op when REBAR_USAGE_LOG unset at usage_log.py:42-44; 'No LLM calls recorded.' at :80-81; per-op + totals table at :94-111; CLI `summarize <path>` at :114-126).

Approach step 2 says 'immediately after result["_usage"] = usage ... call usage_log.record(usage, op=_call_label) (module-level import)' — this is already present: the module-level import is at runner.py:25 (`from rebar.llm import usage_log`) and the call is at runner.py:491 (`usage_log.record(usage, op=_call_label)`).

Approach step 3 says both workflows 'set job env REBAR_USAGE_LOG ... run ... summarize ... >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY ... upload ... via actions/upload-artifact' — already present: external-integration.yml sets REBAR_USAGE_LOG at :137, summarize step (if: always()) at :144-149, upload-artifact at :151-157; prompt-eval.yml sets it at :83, summarize at :107-112, upload-artifact at :113-119.

The plan carries NO `[rebar:<C>]` prerequisite citation on any of these elements, and `show_ticket('usage_log')` returns not-found, so the cited-prerequisite escape does not apply — the assertions fail closed as unbacked against the snapshot.
findings.finding: Every element the plan commits as new/to-be-added already exists in the repo snapshot, so the plan's implicit 'does-not-exist-yet' premise for its whole edit-set is false.
scenarios: An implementer picks up the plan, opens usage_log.py, and finds the 'new' file already written; the runner call and both workflow edits are already committed — the described work is a no-op.
The Acceptance Criteria (all six functional ACs) are vacuously already satisfied against HEAD, so a reviewer cannot tell what delta, if any, this ticket is actually meant to introduce.

findings.finding: The plan sets `REBAR_USAGE_LOG: ${{ runner.temp }}/usage.jsonl` as a job-level `env:` block, but the GitHub Actions `runner` context is unavailable outside step execution — this is a parse-time startup failure that silently produces ZERO jobs, meaning neither weekly job would run at all.
findings.location: Approach section, item 3: 'set job env `REBAR_USAGE_LOG: ${{ runner.temp }}/usage.jsonl`'
findings.suggested_fix:
In each workflow, add an early step:
```yaml
- name: Set usage log path
  run: echo "REBAR_USAGE_LOG=$RUNNER_TEMP/usage.jsonl" >> "$GITHUB_ENV"
```
Then in the summarize/upload steps reference `$REBAR_USAGE_LOG` (now available as a job-level env var propagated via `$GITHUB_ENV`). Alternatively, use the `$RUNNER_TEMP` shell env variable (always available inside `run:` steps) directly in step-level `env:` blocks: `REBAR_USAGE_LOG: ${{ runner.temp }}/usage.jsonl` is valid in a *step-level* `env:` but not a job-level one.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
